evidence: The project experienced bottlenecks in the procurement of rural road and borehole studies.
As concerns the rural roads, services that were initially scheduled to start in April 2004 finally commenced in December 2008 and ended in September 2009, representing a four-year slippage.

During the implementation phase, the project encountered a number of institutional (the project management unit was not provided with its entire staff as planned), economic (the economic crisis led to a depreciation of the dollar and, consequently, of the UA) and financial problems (national counterpart contribution was not raised in time).
It also faced the frequent turnover of project task managers at the Bank.
These difficulties notwithstanding, the outputs achieved at completion are substantial, although all the components were not implemented.

evidence: In general, the transport sector operations financed by the Bank were relatively well executed.

The main implementation problems are: (i) the poor quality of studies; (ii) weak implementation capacity; and (iii) delays in procurement due to poor mastery of Bank rules and procedures, and fiduciary risks.

The main lessons from these projects were factored into the design of the current project as follows: (i) the 2011 studies were updated in 2018; (ii) advance contracting will shorten the procurement timeframes and thus avoid protracted delays between study completion and project execution; (iii) training sessions will be organised for the staff of the project's executing agencies on the Bank's procurement and disbursement rules; and (iv) the Project Management Team at AGEROUTE will rely on the same staff that monitors the fiduciary and procurement components of the Bank's ongoing projects in order to build on the experience they have already acquired.
response.label: No Delay
response.reasoning: The delay-related statements refer to problems encountered in prior transport sector operations, not an actual delay in the current project. The current project is described as incorporating mitigation measures, including advance contracting to avoid protracted delays.

evidence: The rehabilitation of the road was completed in October 2003.

Project 2 loan was approved on 22 October 2008 with actual loan effectiveness on 22 April 2009, ahead of the loan effectiveness date under the loan agreement.
The actual financial closing date was 19 August 2015, a delay of 2 years from the original closing date in the loan agreement.
The delay in financial closing date had four major reasons.

Some bidders had exhibited poor performance in project 1 with late submission of the work program by contractors resulting in mobilization delays.

Construction was more challenging in the hilly terrain and the 18-month completion period was insufficient to complete the works in remote, scattered, and mountainous locations and terrain during a limited working season.
Third, works progressed slowly due to the contractors’ weak capability for planning, project management, and construction management.
Lastly, the occurrence of floods and disasters affected ongoing and completed works and rebidding of three canceled contracts near the loan closing date.
Completed before the original closing date were 11 of 20 contracts while the rest were delayed and necessitated extension.

evidence: Project 1 was approved in October 2007 and became effective in March 2008, 12 days earlier than planned.
The original loan closing date was extended five times, resulting in actual financial closing date of April 2018 or almost 8 years later.
Three factors contributed to the delay in closing.
First, the executing agency lacked experience on ADB’s safeguard policies.

Being the first ADB-financed project with involuntary resettlement and significant land acquisition in Azerbaijan, there was little experience and institutional capacity to handle these issues (PCR, para. 17 and footnote 17).
Second, the project faced a cost overrun requiring a change in scope and an amendment to the loan agreement.
The initial cost estimates completed in 2006 did not factor in the final road alignment, which was not fully finalized as many sources of materials were not identified at that time (PCR, para. 7).
Third, heavy winter rains and the contractor’s poor performance subsequently required further extensions.
The PCR did not provide details on the actual duration of the consulting services.
response.label: Delay
response.reasoning: The excerpt explicitly states that the original loan closing date was extended five times, with actual financial closing almost 8 years later. It also identifies multiple causes of the delay, including safeguard capacity issues, cost overrun and scope amendment, winter rains, and contractor poor performance.

The GoT is committed to addressing the factors that have led to the four transport sector projects being flagged.

Solutions are being implemented to remove the flags from the four projects and to bring them back on track.

evidence: The Project is part of a citywide infrastructural improvement initiative by the Government, which commenced in 2014 under the Kampala Institutional and Infrastructure Development Project (KIIDP).
The implementation of the programme is undertaken in phases.
At present, 42km has been completed with support of the World Bank and the GoU.
The Bank will finance the improvement of 62km while JICA is financing TCC and planned city flyover project as indicated in Table 2.1.

Further, the planned piloting of scheduled bus service in Kampala provides the Government with an opportunity to gradually scale up implementation of higher capacity transit systems in a resource deficient context without significantly disrupting public transport operations in the city.
The project implementation period is assumed to start in June 2020 and completed over a period of 5 years inclusive of one year of defects notification period.

The negative environmental and social impacts associated with the project during the construction phase include: dust pollution from work areas and construction equipment; damage to land and soil arising from materials extraction, spoil disposal, waste disposal from camp sites and work areas as well as disposal of used oil and grease; noise and vibrations; and, public health and safety concerns to workers and general public during construction.

Identified and included mitigation measures to tackle adverse impacts, which have been integrated in the ESMP consist of: (i) strict adherence to restoration and rehabilitation plans for quarries, borrow sites, campsites and other work areas; (ii) contractor to adhere to a mandatory Environmental Management Plan incorporating a comprehensive waste management plan for the construction period developed in consultation with KCCA and NEMA; (iii) enforcement of the occupational health and safety laws and regulations; (iv) sensitization campaigns on gender and HIV/AIDS; and, (v) inclusion of necessary environmental clauses in works contract document to ensure compliance with ESMP requirements.
response.label: No Delay
response.reasoning: The excerpt describes project scope, planned implementation timing, completed portions, expected impacts, and mitigation measures, but contains no evidence of schedule slippage, blocked work, missed milestones, or revised completion dates. The only explicit disruption-related statement says public transport operations would not be significantly disrupted, and no schedule impact is identified.

evidence: The project has been identified as one of the priority road links under the RSDP3.

The Bank is involved in the financing of RSSP 2 - Fort Portal–Bundibugyo–Lamia road (103km) and RSSP3 - Nyakahita–Ibanda– Kamwenge (143km) now extended to Fort Portal
